schools.summary: Chrysostom: after discoursing at large on the love of God toward man and his unspeakable concern, Paul puts forward this exhortation to persuade those who received the benefit to live worthy of the gift, and though so great a person does not decline to beseech them (not for his own gain but theirs); Paul beseeching, by the mercies of God (the gospel motive, Romans 1-11, the love constraining, 2 Corinthians 5:14), to present the body a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable — their 'reasonable service' (reasonable/rational befitting a reasonable creature, or spiritual, of the mind not mere ritual); the whole consecration of the redeemed life (the body devoted, 1 Corinthians 6:20).

sides.position: Reasonable/rational worship, befitting a reasonable creature, and/or spiritual worship of the mind and heart (not mere outward ritual)

themes: present your bodies a living sacrifice
the whole consecration of the redeemed life

schools.summary: Gill: 'this world' may mean the Mosaic dispensation (the worldly sanctuary now passed) or the present evil age with its customs, maxims, and fashions, to which believers are not to conform; be not conformed to this world (the outward fashioning to the world's pattern refused) but be transformed by the renewing of the mind (the inward change of nature, the Spirit renewing the understanding, affections, and will, the metamorphosis of the soul) — the contrast of outward conformity and inward transformation; the renewed mind proving (discerning, testing, approving) the will of God, which is good (beneficial), acceptable (well-pleasing), and perfect (complete).

themes: be not conformed to this world
transformed by the renewing of your mind

schools.summary: Gill: Paul speaks 'through the grace given' — the grace of apostleship, the authority received from Christ to give instructions to the churches; Paul charging every man not to think of himself more highly than he ought, but to think soberly (with sound judgment, modesty, humility), according as God hath dealt the measure of faith — the warning against pride and self-conceit, the call to humble, sober self-estimation (measuring oneself not by self-flattery but by the gifts and faith God has apportioned, 1 Corinthians 4:7, 'what hast thou that thou didst not receive?'), preparing for the teaching on the body (vv.4-5, the humility that becomes the member).

themes: not to think of himself more highly than he ought

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul uses the same illustration as to the Corinthians (1 Corinthians 12) to allay the same passion of haughtiness — great is its power to correct the disease of pride (are we not all one body, both great and small, and members one of another? why then by haughtiness separate thyself?); many members in one body, and not all with the same office — the diversity of the members and their offices grounding both humility (each has his own place, none superior or independent) and unity (many members, one body, members one of another); the natural body the pattern of the church (the spiritual body of Christ, diverse gifts and offices, one body).

themes: all members have not the same office

schools.summary: Gill: the chosen, redeemed, justified, and sanctified, though few in comparison with the world and many in themselves, make up one body, the church, of which Christ is the head; we, being many, are one body in Christ, and every one members one of another — the union of the believers with Christ as the head (the vital union, the head and the body) and with one another as fellow-members (the mutual membership, the believers belonging to one another); the double union (vertical and horizontal) the ground of the love, humility, and service that follow (vv.9-21, members of one body caring for one another), the comfort of the membership in Christ.

themes: we, being many, are one body in Christ
every one members one of another

schools.summary: Gill: as in the natural body the members have different faculties, so in the spiritual body the church the members have diversities of gifts, given according to grace; having gifts differing according to the grace given (not earned but of grace, the free endowments), each to use his — the prophet (preacher, the inspired or expounding ministry) to prophesy 'according to the proportion of faith' (according to the measure of faith/the degree of the gift, or the rule/standard of the faith, the body of truth — prophesying in agreement with the truth, within one's measure); the variety of gifts to follow (vv.6-8), each used in its place.

debates.question: What is 'the proportion of faith' for prophecy?

sides.position: According to the measure of faith given (the degree of the gift) and/or the rule/standard of the faith (the body of truth) — prophesying in agreement with the truth

themes: gifts differing according to the grace
whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith
the diversity of gifts from grace
the right use of the gift

schools.summary: Gill: 'ministry' here may signify the office of ministering to the poor saints (the deacon's office, distinct from prophesying), to be attended with diligence, care, and constancy; let him with the gift of ministry wait on (attend diligently to) his ministering, and the teacher on his teaching — each gift exercised in its own sphere with diligence (the minister serving, the teacher teaching, none neglecting his gift nor intruding on another's, the proper sphere, the order of service), the diligence and constancy required (not slothfully or fitfully but faithfully), the dignity of each gift in the body (both service and instruction needful).

themes: or ministry, let us wait on our ministering
each gift in its own sphere
the diligent use of each gift

schools.summary: Gill: exhortation a branch of preaching, more practical, giving a word of exhortation as cases require (chiefly the pastor's work); Paul completing the list of gifts — the exhorter on exhortation, the giver with simplicity (singleness of heart, sincerity, liberality, without grudging or ostentation), the ruler with diligence (earnest care, not slackness), the one showing mercy with cheerfulness (gladly, not with gloom, 2 Corinthians 9:7) — each gift used not only diligently but in its right spirit (the manner as important as the act, the proper temper crowning the gift, the grace adorning the service).

themes: he that giveth, let him do it with simplicity
he that sheweth mercy, with cheerfulness
the manner as important as the act

schools.summary: Gill: having addressed the officers, Paul now stirs up the exercise of grace and the duties common to all members, beginning with love — the cement of the saints, the bond of perfectness, without which all gifts are nothing; let love be without dissimulation (unfeigned, sincere, not hypocritical, from the heart, 1 Peter 1:22), abhor that which is evil (hate, loathe sin, the moral revulsion) and cleave to that which is good (cling, hold fast to the right) — the sincere love the root of the duties, joined to the moral bent of the renewed heart (v.2, hating what God hates, loving what God loves, the union of love and holiness).

themes: cleave to that which is good

schools.summary: Gill: this a branch of the love just advised — the objects 'brethren,' not in the natural sense but spiritual, born of God, to whom tender, family affection is due; be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love (tenderly loving, the family affection of the saints as brethren born of God) and in honour preferring one another (esteeming others above oneself, giving place, the humility that honours others, Philippians 2:3, 'in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves') — the warm love and the lowly honour of the brotherhood, the bond knitting the saints in affection and esteem.

themes: be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love

schools.summary: Gill: 'business' here means not worldly affairs but spiritual business — the affairs of piety and religion, the service of God, in which we should not be slothful; not slothful in business (diligent, especially in the spiritual life, the earnest application), fervent in spirit (warm, zealous, boiling, not lukewarm or cold, against the deadness that creeps in, Revelation 3:16), serving the Lord (all directed to his service, the Lord the aim and crown) — the threefold charge (diligence against sloth, fervour against coldness, the serving of the Lord the aim), the warm, diligent, God-serving life of the believer.

Gill: 'rejoicing in hope' (of the glory of God) placed between serving the Lord and patience in tribulation — for nothing more animates cheerful service or patience under affliction than the hope of being for ever with the Lord; rejoicing in hope (the joy of the prospect of glory, the cheering hope), patient in tribulation (enduring affliction with steadfast endurance), continuing instant in prayer (constant, persevering, earnest, 1 Thessalonians 5:17, 'pray without ceasing') — the threefold grace upholding the believer through trials (hope cheering, patience bearing, prayer sustaining), the three connected (hope producing patience, patience driving to prayer, prayer renewing hope).

themes: the graces that uphold through trials

schools.summary: Gill: 'distributing' (or communicating) to the necessity of saints — all in want are to be relieved, even enemies, and especially those of the household of faith; distributing to the necessity of saints (relieving the real wants of the poor brethren, sharing one's substance, the liberality to the needy) and given to hospitality (eager for, pursuing it, welcoming and entertaining the saints and strangers, Hebrews 13:2) — the practical charity of the body (the love that meets material need, the love in deed, 1 John 3:17-18, the fellowship of giving and welcoming), 'given to' hospitality meaning more than practising it (pursuing it, seeking to welcome).

themes: distributing to the necessity of saints
the love in giving and welcoming

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having taught how they ought to be minded toward one another and joined the members closely into one, Paul next leads them forth to the battle without (toward those outside), made easier from this point (he who has practised well among his own will more easily deal with strangers); bless them which persecute you, bless and curse not — the returning of blessing for persecution (to pray for, speak well of, and seek the good of the persecutor, not imprecate or revile, the opposite of the natural cursing and revenge, Matthew 5:44) — the hardest of the love-commands, the spirit of Christ toward enemies (who prayed for his murderers, Luke 23:34).

themes: the spirit of Christ toward enemies

schools.summary: Gill: rejoicing with those who rejoice (in good and lawful things, as prosperity) a difficult task — for unless one has a near concern in another's good he is apt to envy or repine; rejoice with them that rejoice and weep with them that weep — the sympathy of the body (entering into one another's joys and sorrows, 1 Corinthians 12:26, 'if one member suffer, all suffer; if one be honoured, all rejoice'), the fellow-feeling that shares the gladness without envy and the grief without indifference (both harder than they seem, the love that makes another's joy and sorrow its own).

themes: rejoice with them that do rejoice

schools.summary: Gill: 'be of the same mind' not sameness of judgment in every point but having the same love, the same accord and affection, esteeming others better than oneself; be of the same mind (the harmony of mutual affection), mind not high things but condescend to men of low estate (not aspiring to the great but stooping to/associating with the lowly, the poor, the obscure), be not wise in your own conceits (not trusting one's own wisdom or despising others' counsel, Proverbs 3:7) — the threefold charge against pride (harmony against discord, condescension against ambition, modesty against self-conceit), the lowliness that becomes the members of the one body.

themes: be of the same mind one toward another
condescend to men of low estate
be not wise in your own conceits
the lowliness that becomes the body

schools.summary: Gill: 'recompense to no man evil for evil' — neither evil words for evil words (railing for railing), nor evil deeds for evil deeds (one ill turn for another); private revenge here forbidden, though the magistrate may punish evil (Romans 13:4, the distinction between private vengeance forbidden and the magistrate's lawful office); the believer not returning injury for injury (the spirit of Christ, 1 Peter 2:23), and providing things honest in the sight of all men (taking thought for honourable, becoming conduct, giving no just occasion of reproach, the blameless walk before the world) — the no-revenge and the honest walk joined.

themes: recompense to no man evil for evil
provide things honest in the sight of all men

schools.summary: Barnes: 'if it be possible' implies that it cannot always be done (peace depends on others too), still it should be an object of desire; 'as much as lieth in you' implies we are to do our utmost to preserve peace and to appease the anger and malice of others; the believer to live peaceably with all men — the pursuit of peace qualified by the two limits ('if it be possible,' for peace is not always attainable and not to be bought at the cost of truth or conscience; 'as much as lieth in you,' the believer's part to do all he can, the responsibility owned), the duty of peaceableness sought as far as conscience and others allow.

themes: if it be possible... live peaceably with all men
as much as lieth in you
peace as far as conscience and others allow

schools.summary: Gill: the affectionate 'dearly beloved' used to move them (they were dear to Paul and beloved of God); avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath ('give place' chiefly meaning leave room for God's wrath, commit the matter to his righteous judgment; some take it as giving place to the adversary's wrath, yielding), for 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord' (Deuteronomy 32:35, God reserving vengeance to himself, the believer not to usurp it) — the renouncing of self-vengeance, the trust that commits the wrong to God the righteous Judge (1 Peter 2:23), the comfort that God will repay.

debates.question: What does 'give place unto wrath' mean?

sides.position: Give place to God's wrath — leave room for God to avenge, committing the matter to his righteous judgment (chief sense)
Give place to the adversary's wrath — yield, do not resist

themes: avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath
Vengeance is mine; I will repay
the wrong committed to the Judge

schools.summary: Gill: these words taken from Proverbs 25:21, to be understood (as a Jewish writer observes) in their literal sense — so far should the saints be from revenging themselves that they should do good to their enemies; if thine enemy hunger feed him, if he thirst give him drink, for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head — the positive love returning good for evil (beyond non-revenge, v.19, to active good); 'coals of fire' chiefly the kindness that melts the enemy to shame and repentance (softening him, the love that wins), or, if he remains hardened, leaving him the more inexcusable (the guilt aggravated); the overcoming of the enemy by kindness (v.21).

debates.question: What does 'heap coals of fire on his head' mean?

sides.position: The kindness, like coals, melts the enemy to shame and repentance (the chief, kindest sense)
If unmelted, the kindness leaves the enemy the more inexcusable, aggravating his guilt

themes: if thine enemy hunger, feed him
heap coals of fire on his head
the kindness that may melt him to repentance

schools.summary: Gill: the believer not to be overcome by the evil one (Satan, who stirs up revenge), nor by the evil of sin within, nor by the evil of others' injuries, but to resist and conquer; be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good — the grand principle crowning the section (vv.14-21), the believer not conquered by the evil done to him (driven to revenge, bitterness, retaliation, hatred) but conquering the evil by returning good (answering it with kindness, melting the enemy, v.20, or keeping himself from being drawn into evil) — the triumph of good over evil, the Christian victory.

themes: the triumph of good over evil
